title: Autodesk Maya File Import-Export (.mb .ma)
description:
Autodesk Maya File Import-Export (.mb .ma) 

🧩 Overview

This powerful and user-friendly plugin streamlines the workflow between Blender and Autodesk Maya, allowing you to directly import and export .mb and .ma files within the Blender interface—without manually launching Maya.

It leverages Maya’s Python environment (mayapy.exe) in the background to read and convert Maya-native assets. As such, a local installation of Autodesk Maya is required, but Maya itself does not need to be opened during the process.


🎯 Supported Asset Types

  • 3D Models: High-fidelity geometry and UV transfer with smooth cross-platform compatibility.

  • Animation: Supports both rigged and keyframe-based animations.

  • Materials: Transfers standard materials via FBX; complex Maya shader networks may need manual adjustment.

  • Character Assets: Includes rigged meshes, skeletons, and skinning data for further editing and rendering in Blender (via FBX conversion).

  • Cameras & Lights: Basic camera parameters and light setups are preserved.



⚙️ System Requirements & Setup

  • Operating System: Windows

  • Required Software: Blender 3.0+ and Autodesk Maya 2018 or newer (must be installed)

  • Setup: In the plugin preferences, specify the path to Maya’s Python interpreter (mayapy.exe)

  • Non-Intrusive: Maya does not need to be opened—conversion happens entirely in the background.


💡 Who It's For

Ideal for artists, animators, and studios working in both Maya and Blender, this plugin:

  • Reduces tedious export/import steps

  • Maintains fidelity across formats

  • Preserves the integrity of creative assets during transfer

💝 Community Contribution

We proudly donate a portion of this plugin’s proceeds each month to the Blender Foundation, supporting the future of open-source 3D creation. Your purchase helps fuel innovation in the creative community.

🔎 Key Differences from Other DCC Bridges

  • Maya Installation Required: Unlike C4D workflows that can bypass installation, this plugin relies on mayapy.exe for native file access, so Maya must be installed.

  • Windows-Only (for now): Current implementation includes hardcoded Windows paths (e.g., C:\\Program Files\\Autodesk\\Maya2025\\...). For macOS/Linux support, cross-platform path detection or manual config is required.

  • Realistic Phrasing: Rather than calling it “revolutionary,” we emphasize practical simplification due to its dependency on external software.
documentation:
Autodesk Maya File Import-Export Add-on

User Guide for Blender (v 2.0.1)

1. Overview

Autodesk Maya File Import-Export lets you read and write native Maya .mb / .ma files directly inside Blender.

Workflow in a nutshell

  1. Blender side – Selected objects are exported to a temporary FBX.

  2. Maya Standalone (mayapy) – The FBX is converted to/from a Maya file without launching the Maya UI.

  3. Blender side – The final file is automatically imported or saved.

Key benefits

  • No need to open Maya’s GUI

  • Granular filters (Meshes, Lights, Cameras, Splines, Animations, Materials, Armatures)

  • Drag-and-drop import, batch conversion, detailed logging, automatic cache cleanup

2. System Requirements

Component Minimum / Recommended
Blender 4.2.0 + (Windows / macOS / Linux)
Autodesk Maya 2018 – 2025 with mayapy installed
Disk space ≥ 2 GB free (temporary FBX + log cache)

3. Installation

  1. Download the ZIP from GitHub or Blender Market.

  2. Blender → Edit › Preferences › Add-ons › Install… → pick the ZIP → Install Add-on.

  3. Enable Autodesk Maya File Import-Export.

  4. In the add-on preferences, set the path to mayapy:

    • Default (Windows):
      C:\Program Files\Autodesk\Maya2025\bin\mayapy.exe

    • macOS / Linux: choose the corresponding mayapy executable.

4. Exporting from Blender to Maya

  1. Select the objects you want to export.

  2. File › Export › Autodesk Maya (.mb, .ma).

  3. Tick the asset types to include (Meshes, Lights, etc.).

  4. Choose a file name and extension (.mb or .ma) → Export Maya File.

  5. Progress and errors are written to ~/Documents/blender_maya_log.txt.

Tip – To export animation only, tick Animations (and optionally Armatures) and untick everything else.

5. Importing from Maya to Blender

Method A – Menu

  1. File › Import › Autodesk Maya (.mb, .ma).

  2. Pick a .mb or .ma file.

  3. In the right-hand panel choose what to import.

  4. Enable Maya Axis (180 ° X-flip) if you work in Y-Up in Blender.

  5. Import Maya File.

Method B – Drag & Drop

Drag the .mb / .ma file into the 3D Viewport or Outliner → configure options → OK.

6. Option Reference

Checkbox / Field Icon Meaning (Export ≈ Import)
Models 🞇 Mesh objects
Lights 💡 Light objects
Cameras 📷 Cameras
Splines ➰ Curves / splines
Animations 🎞️ Keyframes & bone animation
Materials † 🎨 Keep material slots
Armatures † 🦴 Skeleton / bone data
Maya Axis † — Rotate +180 ° around X (Y-Up ↔ Z-Up)

† Import-only options.

7. Cache & Logging

Path Purpose
~/Documents/blender_maya_cache Temporary FBX files (auto-deletes)
~/Documents/blender_maya_log.txt Full addon + Maya Standalone log

Check the log first when something goes wrong.

8. FAQ

Issue Likely Cause Fix
“mayapy not found” Wrong path / Maya not installed Re-enter correct path in Preferences
Export freezes at 0 % Unsupported objects / no write perms Export only needed assets; check folder permissions
Imported model is upside-down Axis mismatch Enable Maya Axis on import or rotate manually
Log shows fbxmaya.mll error Maya FBX plugin missing Load fbxmaya.mll in Maya or reinstall Maya

9. Best Practices

  • Coordinate systems – Decide on Y-Up vs Z-Up and stick to it; use Maya Axis when needed.

  • Import only what you need – Speeds up conversion and keeps files small.

  • Clean the cache periodically if you batch-convert many files.

  • Adjust FBX version in the script if you need legacy compatibility.

10. Changelog (v 2.0.1)

  • Drag-and-drop import dialog

  • Per-asset filters for Materials & Armatures

  • Enhanced log formatting with error tags

  • Automatic deletion of temporary FBX files

11. Support & Feedback

When filing a bug, include:

  1. Blender and Maya version

  2. OS and GPU info

  3. Relevant excerpt from blender_maya_log.txt

Happy shuttling between Blender and Maya!
